text: The surgeon begins the procedure by taking down adhesions, carefully separating multiple loops of small bowel that are stuck in the pelvis.
The surgeon performs cold scissor dissection, carefully cutting through tissue without the use of cautery.
The surgeon removes tissue from the pelvis to expose and identify the malignant fistula, ensuring clear visualization of the affected area.
The surgeon has successfully maneuvered one loop of bowel down, likely in preparation for further dissection or resection.
The surgeon identifies a second loop of bowel adhered to the area surrounding the fistula site.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the small bowel out of the pelvis, leaving the colon, fistula, and implant in the pelvic region.
The implant is positioned close to the internal iliac vessels on the patient's left side, indicating its proximity to these anatomical structures.
The surgeon begins the mobilization of the colon, preparing to separate it from surrounding tissues in the pelvic region.
The surgeon begins the procedure with a lateral approach to mobilize the colon, focusing on the anatomical structures involved in the colectomy.
The surgeon observes the fused tissue in the surgical field, indicating a challenging dissection area.
The surgeon carefully progresses down towards the fistula, taking time to navigate through the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues to dissect and approach the area surrounding the fistula, carefully manipulating the tissue to access the affected region.
The surgeon continues dissection, guided by the visible plane and ICG imaging, ensuring the ureter is not in proximity.
The surgeon encounters granulation tissue, suggesting proximity to the area of the fistula.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to dissect and retract tissue, maneuvering the instrument to separate and expose anatomical structures during the colectomy.
The surgeon clears smoke and blood from the surgical field, improving visibility for continued dissection around the fistula area.
The surgeon shifts focus to a different area in an attempt to find a more stable tissue plane for dissection.
The surgeon identifies a stable tissue plane and follows it down into the pelvis, facilitating further dissection in the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the ureter, clearly visible in the surgical field during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon increases the mobility of the bowel, allowing for better manipulation and visualization of the point of fixation.
The surgeon exposes the tumor within the surgical field, revealing its location and surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies an implant in the lateral mesorectum extending towards the location of the fistula.
The surgeon has partially incised into the tumor, revealing its internal structure.
The surgeon maneuvers around the exposed tumor to access and prepare for the removal of the fistula, ensuring clear visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a small dissection plane adjacent to the tumor within the lateral mesorectum, preparing to further separate tissue layers.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field near the iliac vessels, initially mistaking the area for the lateral aspect of the mesorectum.
The surgeon continues dissection beneath the mesorectum over the sacral promontory, carefully navigating the anatomical space.
The surgeon performs a posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, carefully separating the mesorectal tissue from the surrounding structures in the pelvis.
The surgeon attempts to extend the posterior dissection around the right lateral aspect in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, aiming to connect it anteriorly.
The surgeon continues the dissection beneath the mesorectum over the sacral promontory, achieving better delineation of the anatomical structures in the TME plane.
The surgeon works directly anteriorly in the rectovaginal septum, focusing on the dissection within this anatomical area.
The surgeon continues dissection in the fat plane within the pouch of Douglas, maintaining the surgical plane during the colectomy.
The surgeon navigates the dissection towards the area of the metastasis, utilizing the previously established posterior dissection path around the right lateral aspect in the TME plane.
The surgeon dissects in the rectovaginal septum, revealing the mobility of the bowel as it is lifted and separated within the fat plane of the pouch of Douglas.
The surgeon observes clear yellow fat, potentially identifying it as mesorectum during the dissection process.
The surgeon continues dissection in the anterior plane, with visible mobility of the bowel, while avoiding entry into the vaginal area.
The surgeon repositions to approach the surgical field from a posterior angle, adjusting the view to continue the dissection.
The surgeon joins the left-sided dissection, continuing to work in the anterior region with visible yellow fat, likely mesorectum, in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the tumor as the remaining structure adhered to the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon inadvertently pulls lateral tissue in an anterior direction during the procedure.
The surgeon navigates around the ureters, which are visible within the yellow fat, carefully maneuvering to avoid the metastatic tissue.
The surgeon identifies a vein in the surgical field, noting that it is a branch of the left internal iliac vein.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Prolene suture, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a stitch in the rectovaginal septum, visible in the anterior region of the surgical field.

The surgeon demonstrates the distortion caused by pulling, showing the invasive metastasis into the lateral iliac chain tissue. The vein is visibly pulled over, revealing the surgeon's position on the left aspect of the iliac chain rather than anteriorly.
The surgeon identifies the fatty plane laterally and proceeds to mobilize the mesorectum circumferentially distal to the area, enhancing tissue mobility.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer instead of scissors to manage potential iliac bleeding, addressing peripheral branches that may need to be sacrificed.
The surgeon encounters very thickened and edematous tissue during the procedure.
The surgeon observes the junction where the posterior plane meets the lateral plane, providing a clear view of the anatomical structures during the dissection.
The surgeon observes the lateral plane clearly, indicating the visible anatomical separation between tissue layers during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to address the thickened and edematous tissue, avoiding the use of scissors in the current dissection.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the instrument closely along the surface of the tumor, maintaining precise contact with the tumor's boundary.
The surgeon visualizes the stitch placed on the internal iliac artery.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the vessel sealer close to the tumor, ensuring precise separation without inadvertently grasping the iliac vessels.
The surgeon successfully removes the metastatic tumor from the sidewall and proceeds to dissect into the distal lateral mesorectum.
The surgeon visually inspects the ureters to ensure they are intact and unharmed during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the lateral part of the mesorectum, ensuring the tissue is fully separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon cleans the surgical field, preparing to approach anteriorly for stapling.
The surgeon identifies the stitch marking the location of the iliac vessels, which are positioned anteriorly and distal to the tumor and fistula.
The surgeon inspects the pelvic region, checking for any signs of bleeding.
The surgeon removes a small, free-floating piece of tumor from the surgical field.
The surgeon visually inspects the iliac vessels, checking their position and condition in relation to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon trims the iliac suture, adjusting the length and ensuring it is properly positioned.
